title: The First Practical Decision: Funeral Home or Medical Examiner
summary: One of the earliest decisions is who takes the person into their care — and it depends mostly on the circumstances of the death.
category: Death of a Loved One (Transition into After-a-Loss)
journeyStage: Death of a Loved One (Transition into After-a-Loss)
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: The First Practical Decision: Funeral Home or Medical Examiner | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: One of the earliest decisions is who takes the person into their care — and it depends mostly on the circumstances of the death.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Most deaths (expected, under medical care) go directly to a funeral home of your choosing.
Sudden or unattended deaths may involve the medical examiner or coroner first.
You are allowed to take time to choose a funeral home, even under pressure to decide quickly.

paragraphs: For most expected deaths, family or hospice can call a funeral home directly, who will come take the person into their care — this can be a funeral home the family already knows, or one found through a quick search or hospital referral.

In cases of sudden, unattended, or unexplained death, the medical examiner or coroner's office may need to be involved first, sometimes requiring an autopsy, before the person is released to a funeral home. This process is procedural, not a judgment about the death.

nextSteps: Ask the hospital, hospice, or 911 responders whether the medical examiner needs to be involved.
Take time choosing a funeral home rather than deciding under pressure.
Ask the first funeral home you call what happens next, step by step.
disclaimer: Processes and requirements vary by state and circumstance — the responding professionals (911, hospice, hospital staff) can guide the specific next step.
